title: practical english usage - Part 366
content:
2  well     = 'in  good    health' between the verb and the object, ►     19 6.1 .) Note that we  cannot say She speaks well English. (Adverbs cannot usually go Her English is good.

She speaks good English.

- She speaks English well. (     S     speaks English good.) I like that teacher. He’s good. (NOT    He’s well.) - He teaches very well.

It’s a good car. (adjective modifying car) It's a well-made car. (adverb modifying made) not - The car runs well. (adverb modifying runs) (     The car runs good.) while good is an adjective. Compare: Well and good can have similar meanings, but in this case well is an adverb, 1  well     and   good 622well London - you’re welcome to it! (= I hope you like it more than I do.) Welcome to London!

It's more trouble than it’s worth.' ‘ Aren't you worried that somebody will steal your car? ‘ They’re welcome to it.

Note the common negative meaning of 'welcome to it'.

1 .

For by the way, ►  301.

We'll have lunch on our way. Spring is on the way.

On  the/my, etc way means ‘ during the journey/movement' or ‘ coming’ .

Please don’ t stand in the doorway - you're in my way.

I can’t get the car out because those boxes are in the way.

things that stop people getting where they want to.

These expressions are quite different. In the/my, etc way is used for obstacles - 5 in   the    way     and   on   the    way Word  Problems from A  to Z     Section     31 vocabulary •  623   when and if Which/ What people jiave influenced you most in your life?

Which/ What train did you come on?

Which/ What is the hottest city in the world?

Which and what are often both possible, with little difference of meaning.

1 which       and   what 625 which,       what      and   who:    question       words 233.1 For where in relative clauses, ► ‘ Could you send this off for me? ‘ Where to?

To is not normally dropped in the short question Where to?

Where are you going (to)? Where does this road lead (to)?

To is often dropped after where.

624 where       (to) When/If I'm in Liverpool I usually stay with my sister.

When! If you heat ice it turns to water.

'whenever'), both when and if can be used with little difference of meaning.

To  talk about repeated, predictable situations and events (in the sense of perhaps not.) I'll see you in August if I come to New York. (Perhaps I’ ll come  to New  York, at Sophie’ s place.) I'll see you at Christmas when we're all at Sophie's place. (We are certain to be happen. A  person who  says if is unsure whether it will happen. Tm good is now often used informally to mean 'I'm OK',  especially when 3 I'm  good.

vocabulary •         who and whom With whom did she go? (very formal) Prepositions normally come  before whom.

Whom  did they arrest? (formal) In a very formal style, whom is sometimes used.

Who did she go with ?

Prepositions usually come at the end of u/fto-questions (►  20$)).

Who did they arrest?

We   normally use who as an object in questions.

1 questions:      Who      did    they     arrest?

Whom  is unusual in informal modern English.

However, which can be used in questions about people’ s identity, and what can Who are you going out with - Lesley or Maria?
